title: The Count of Monte Cristo
author: Dumas, Alexandre
summary: On 24 February 1815 the merchantman Pharaon arrives at Marseille. Edmond Dantès, a composed young mate, skillfully manages the vessel after Captain Leclere’s sudden death by fever at sea. He reports the cargo safe to shipowner Morrel, buries Leclere at sea with naval honors, and follows the late captain’s orders to touch at Elba to deliver a packet. Dantès recounts meeting Marshal Bertrand and the emperor briefly. Morrel praises Dantès’s seamanship and hints at promotion; Danglars, the supercargo, resents him. The crew obeys Dantès; health and customs officers board. Tension arises over the Elba visit and the mysterious packet. still

characters.name: Edmond Dantès
characters.lifespan: Survives the voyage; assumes command as mate and delivers packet; remains alive at the end of the excerpt

characters.name: Captain Leclere
characters.lifespan: Dies of a fever at sea; buried sewn in his hammock with a shot at head and heels

characters.name: M. Morrel (Morrel & Son)
characters.lifespan: Alive in the excerpt; praises Dantès and considers his promotion

characters.name: M. Danglars
characters.personality: obsequious to superiors, insolent to subordinates, jealous
characters.lifespan: Alive; serves as supercargo and harbors resentment toward Dantès

characters.name: Marshal Bertrand
characters.lifespan: Recipient/associate mentioned in packet delivery; alive in the excerpt

characters.name: The Emperor (Napoleon)
characters.personality: briefly inquisitive and observant in encounter
characters.lifespan: Appears briefly to Dantès at Elba; alive within the excerpt

characters.name: Policar Morrel
characters.lifespan: Mentioned as Morrel’s uncle and remembered by the emperor; alive in the excerpt

characters.name: Pilot (unnamed)
characters.lifespan: Alive; boards and steers the Pharaon into port

characters.name: Crew / All hands
characters.lifespan: Alive; obey Dantès's orders and perform maneuvers

characters.name: Health officers / Customs inspectors
characters.lifespan: Board the vessel at Marseille to perform inspections; alive in excerpt

characters.name: Captain Leclere's widow
characters.lifespan: Receives Captain Leclere’s sword and cross of honor as mentioned

unusual_words.definition: a sailor stationed to keep watch
unusual_words.context: The look-out at Notre-Dame de la Garde signalled the three-master.

unusual_words.definition: a ship with three masts
unusual_words.context: The Pharaon is described as a three-master from Smyrna, Trieste, and Naples.

unusual_words.definition: proper name of the ship in the narrative
unusual_words.context: The merchantman Pharaon arrives at Marseille.

unusual_words.definition: indicated by a lookout or signal
unusual_words.context: The look-out signalled the Pharaon’s arrival.

unusual_words.definition: to turn a ship around a point or cape
unusual_words.context: A pilot put off and rounding the Château d'If got on board.

unusual_words.definition: small island fortress off Marseille
unusual_words.context: The pilot boarded the Pharaon between Cape Morgiou and Rion island near the Château d’If.

unusual_words.definition: defensive walls of a fortification
unusual_words.context: The ramparts of Fort Saint-Jean were covered with spectators.

unusual_words.definition: historic fort in Marseille
unusual_words.context: Spectators lined the ramparts of Fort Saint-Jean to watch the arrival.

unusual_words.definition: sails set above the lowermost sails on a mast
unusual_words.context: The ship approached harbor under topsails, jib, and spanker.

unusual_words.definition: a triangular sail set forward of the foremast
unusual_words.context: The Pharaon entered the port under jib and spanker.

unusual_words.definition: a sail set on the aftermost mast (aft sail)
unusual_words.context: The vessel approached under topsails, jib, and spanker.

unusual_words.definition: people who stand around with nothing to do; onlookers
unusual_words.context: Idlers along the ramparts speculated about misfortune on board.

unusual_words.definition: something that precedes and indicates the approach of something else
unusual_words.context: Idlers had that instinct which is the forerunner of evil.

unusual_words.definition: a state of unease or anxiety
unusual_words.context: A vague disquietude prevailed among the spectators.

unusual_words.definition: a small light boat
unusual_words.context: A man jumped into a small skiff to reach the Pharaon.

unusual_words.definition: beside or next to (a ship)
unusual_words.context: The man desired to be pulled alongside the Pharaon.

unusual_words.definition: a basin/area of Marseille harbor referenced in the book
unusual_words.context: The Pharaon reached La Réserve basin where the skiff came alongside.

unusual_words.definition: the sides of a ship above the deck
unusual_words.context: Dantès leaned over the ship’s bulwarks to speak with M. Morrel.

unusual_words.definition: a feeling of deep sadness
unusual_words.context: Dantès had a melancholy smile while speaking of the captain’s death.

unusual_words.definition: an old term for a severe fever affecting the brain (encephalitic symptoms)
unusual_words.context: Captain Leclere died of brain-fever in dreadful agony.

unusual_words.definition: stitched up; here meaning prepared for burial at sea
unusual_words.context: The captain was sewn up in his hammock for burial at sea.

unusual_words.definition: a weight of shot (cannonball) used as a sea burial anchor
unusual_words.context: A thirty-six-pound shot was placed at his head and heels for burial.

unusual_words.definition: the act of burying; funeral rites
unusual_words.context: They performed the usual burial service and consigned him to the sea.

unusual_words.definition: officer on a merchant ship second to the captain
unusual_words.context: Edmond Dantès is the mate who assumes command after the captain’s death.

unusual_words.definition: heavy device used to moor a ship
unusual_words.context: The anchor was dropped and the chain ran rattling through the port-hole.

unusual_words.definition: nautical term describing an anchor set with its arm raised (at an angle)
unusual_words.context: The anchor a-cockbill indicated the ship rode at anchor properly.

unusual_words.definition: spar projecting from the bowsprit to support jibs
unusual_words.context: The ship bore evidence of being skilfully handled: the jib-boom guys eased off.

unusual_words.definition: lines used to gather up a sail to the yard or mast
unusual_words.context: Hands sprang to spanker brails and outhaul when lowering sails.

unusual_words.definition: a line used to tension the foot of a sail
unusual_words.context: Crew adjusted the spanker brails and outhaul as ordered.

unusual_words.definition: the lines controlling the lower corners of the topsail
unusual_words.context: Crewman handled the topsail sheets when taking in sail.

unusual_words.definition: lines used to hoist sails, flags, or yards
unusual_words.context: Halyards were handled among the topsail and jib preparations.

unusual_words.definition: a line used to haul down the jib
unusual_words.context: The crew operated the jib downhaul during mooring.

unusual_words.definition: lines used to haul up the clews (lower corners) of a topsail
unusual_words.context: They worked the topsail clewlines and buntlines in concert.

unusual_words.definition: lines used to gather up a square sail into the bunt
unusual_words.context: Buntlines helped stow the topsail as the ship neared harbor.

unusual_words.definition: agent responsible for cargo and commercial matters aboard ship
unusual_words.context: Danglars is the supercargo who addresses the owner about the voyage.

unusual_words.definition: excessively obedient or attentive to superiors
unusual_words.context: Danglars is described as obsequious to his superiors.

unusual_words.definition: not particularly attractive or imposing in appearance
unusual_words.context: Danglars is said to have an unprepossessing countenance.

unusual_words.definition: rude or disrespectful
unusual_words.context: Danglars is insolent to subordinates, making him disliked by the crew.

unusual_words.definition: those in lower rank or position
unusual_words.context: Danglars behaves insolently toward subordinates aboard.

unusual_words.definition: skill in managing a ship and handling its navigation and sails
unusual_words.context: Morrel praises Dantès’s seamanship as evidence of fitness for command.

unusual_words.definition: carried out orders; complied
unusual_words.context: All hands obeyed promptly when Dantès issued commands.

unusual_words.definition: command to release the anchor
unusual_words.context: Dantès orders, 'Let go—and clue up!' during mooring.

unusual_words.definition: to clew up the sail (bring up the clews); furling order
unusual_words.context: The command 'Let go—and clue up!' is given while preparing sails.

unusual_words.definition: a flag position lowered to half its usual height as a sign of mourning
unusual_words.context: Dantès orders 'Half-mast the colors' for the captain’s death.

unusual_words.definition: to align yards across the ship to steady it or set sails square
unusual_words.context: Dantès instructs to 'square the yards' as part of anchoring.

unusual_words.definition: a small sealed letter or parcel entrusted for delivery
unusual_words.context: Captain Leclere gave Dantès a packet to deliver to Marshal Bertrand.

unusual_words.definition: historic spelling of Portoferraio on Elba
unusual_words.context: Dantès left a packet at Porto-Ferrajo as instructed.

unusual_words.definition: island off the Tuscan coast where Napoleon was exiled in 1814–1815
unusual_words.context: The Pharaon touched at Elba to deliver the packet and encountered the emperor.

unusual_words.definition: Civitavecchia, a port town north-west of Rome
unusual_words.context: The burial at sea of Captain Leclere took place off El Giglio after leaving Civita Vecchia.

unusual_words.definition: Isola del Giglio, island off the Tuscan coast
unusual_words.context: They buried the captain at sea off El Giglio island.

unusual_words.definition: a cannonball; used here as weight in burial at sea
unusual_words.context: A thirty-six-pound shot was placed with the captain’s body.

unusual_words.definition: making a rapid succession of short, sharp sounds
unusual_words.context: The anchor chain ran rattling through the port-hole.

unusual_words.definition: an opening or hatch through which gear or chain passes
unusual_words.context: The anchor chain rattled through the port-hole as the anchor was dropped.

unusual_words.definition: official in charge of the harbor’s operations
unusual_words.context: Dantès had spoken with the harbor-master at Naples.

unusual_words.definition: reference to Phocaea/Phocaean docks (ancient Greek colony influence on Marseille)
unusual_words.context: The Pharaon had been built, rigged, and laden at the old Phocee docks.

unusual_words.definition: commercial firm or company
unusual_words.context: The Pharaon belonged to the house of Morrel & Son.

unusual_words.definition: person who owns the ship or business
unusual_words.context: M. Morrel, the owner, converses with Dantès about the voyage.

unusual_words.definition: a body of troops stationed at a particular location
unusual_words.context: Policar Morrel served in the same regiment when the emperor was in garrison at Valence.

unusual_words.definition: an old French exclamation meaning 'by God' or 'indeed'
unusual_words.context: Morrel exclaims 'Pardieu!' when surprised by the emperor’s memory of his uncle.

unusual_words.definition: restlessness or eagerness
unusual_words.context: Morrel’s impatience is observed as he wishes to come on board quickly.

unusual_words.definition: a passage for boarding or leaving a ship
unusual_words.context: Dantès departs to the gangway when health officers and customs inspectors come alongside.

unusual_words.definition: the act of dropping an anchor to moor a ship
unusual_words.context: Dantès attends to the anchoring and orders the ship dressed in mourning.

unusual_words.definition: expression of sorrow (for the dead), often by lowering flags
unusual_words.context: Dantès intends to dress the ship in mourning for the deceased captain.

unusual_words.definition: old/alternative spelling of 'maneuver', meaning a planned movement or operation
unusual_words.context: The manœuvre of lowering sails was executed promptly by the crew.

unusual_words.definition: watching or noting attentively
unusual_words.context: Dantès, observing the owner’s impatience, invites him aboard.

unusual_words.definition: accepting something undesirable but inevitable
unusual_words.context: Morrel shows considerable resignation when learning of the captain’s death.

unusual_words.definition: a sudden or capricious idea or desire
unusual_words.context: Danglars accuses Dantès of losing time at Elba from pure whim.

unusual_words.definition: comforted in sorrow or distress
unusual_words.context: Morrel appears comforted as he reflects on mortality and promotion.

unusual_words.definition: to take on a role or position
unusual_words.context: Dantès assumed command after the captain’s death as mate.

unusual_words.definition: one in a higher rank or position
unusual_words.context: Danglars is obsequious to his superiors but insolent to those below him.

unusual_words.definition: to become aware or realize
unusual_words.context: Experienced navigators perceived the vessel was well handled despite slow approach.

unusual_words.definition: a striking or impressive sight
unusual_words.context: A ship entering port is always a spectacle for Marseille’s spectators.

unusual_words.definition: to speak informally with another
unusual_words.context: Dantès converses with Morrel and later with the emperor.

unusual_words.definition: with keen or intense desire
unusual_words.context: The owner inquires eagerly about the cargo upon learning of the captain’s death.

unusual_words.definition: an authoritative order
unusual_words.context: Dantès issues commands to the crew to take in sails and anchor.

unusual_words.definition: without delay; immediately
unusual_words.context: The crew responded promptly to Dantès’s orders.

unusual_words.definition: consideration in dealing with others; diplomacy (implied)
unusual_words.context: Dantès shows tact in explaining the Elba call and packet to Morrel.

unusual_words.definition: harbor or place where ships dock
unusual_words.context: The Pharaon draws into port after rounding Pomègue and the strait.

unusual_words.definition: a narrow passage of water connecting two seas or two areas of water
unusual_words.context: The vessel had safely passed the strait between Calasareigne and Jaros islands.

unusual_words.definition: one of the islands named in the text (historical/local name)
unusual_words.context: A volcanic shock has made a strait between the Calasareigne and Jaros islands.

unusual_words.definition: one of the islands named in the text (historical/local name)
unusual_words.context: The strait between the Calasareigne and Jaros islands is referenced as passed by the Pharaon.

unusual_words.definition: one of the Frioul islands near Marseille
unusual_words.context: The Pharaon had doubled Pomègue before approaching the harbor.

unusual_words.definition: a low or subdued expression or remark (implied usage)
unusual_words.context: A murmur of vague disquietude passed among observers at the slow approach.

unusual_words.definition: well-formed or admirable in appearance
unusual_words.context: Dantès is described as a fine, tall, slim young fellow with a vigilant eye.

unusual_words.definition: watchful and alert to danger or irregularity
unusual_words.context: Dantès watches every motion of the ship with a vigilant eye.
